citations.text: Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with documented generalised spike-and-wave activity on prior EEG (2017)
Current Medication: Sodium valproate 500 mg bd; Clobazam 10 mg nocte (as rescue on cluster days)
Maintain clobazam 10 mg at night for up to 3 nights during cluster periods, as per existing protocol
Over the last four weeks, the records note: "Cluster days twice this month; typically six seizures in 24 h."
These events are brief generalised convulsions with rapid recovery, no new focal features, and no injuries reported.
He describes a prodrome of diffuse head pressure and photophobia on days preceding clusters, occasionally with poor sleep the night before.
There is no clear relation to intercurrent illness, but stress and missed meals were mentioned as possible triggers.
Where rescue has been needed, clobazam 10 mg nocte has been used for up to three consecutive nights with good effect and without excessive daytime sedation.
Liver function tests in August were within normal limits
He remains seizure‑free between clusters for prolonged stretches, and there have been no emergency department attendances this year.

citations.exact_quote: She is known to our service with focal epilepsy
reports a steady improvement over recent months, which she attributes to better adherence following use of a consistent seizure diary and reminders on her phone.
Current treatment consists of Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d., both well tolerated without dose-limiting adverse effects.
In February she experienced a prolonged focal seizure lasting 8 minutes, resolving without intervention.
In May there were four further brief absences
in August a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure was reported at school (not witnessed by family).
These episodes have been clearly recorded in her diary, with triggers likely related to missed meals and sleep deprivation around coursework deadlines.
Since adopting structured reminders in late August, she has had no further events and has taken all doses on time.
There is no current concern regarding mood or sleep beyond occasional pre-examination anxiety, which she manages with standard breathing techniques taught by our epilepsy nurse specialist.
Neurological examination today is unremarkable. There are no drug-related rashes, no tremor, and no cognitive concerns on bedside screening.
We discussed safety and rescue planning; no rescue medication has been required.
Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. unchanged.
Bloods today for routine monitoring (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) – to be copied to you.
Overall, her focal epilepsy appears better controlled with improved adherence

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Patient has generalised epilepsy.

Present Medication:

1. Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily
2. Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for jet-lag periods (patient-reported intermittent use)

Present Seizure Frequency:

Two events over the last five months.
he is a frequent business traveller with irregular sleep and time zones.
He reports adherence to valproate on most days but occasionally misses doses during long-haul flights.
His first seizure occurred in May 2019 in Ireland, at night while asleep. He woke with rhythmic twitching of the right arm and a sense of déjà vu.
The second event was in October 2019 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms.
He denies daytime collapses, injuries, or prolonged postictal confusion.
No alcohol excess reported; significant sleep disruption around both events.
Arrange routine EEG at next in-person visit when in London; if logistics difficult, consider ambulatory EEG coordinated around his travel.

citations.text: Regarding current events, he still has focal onset seizures four times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change.
He and his partner describe the focal episodes as brief lapses with a rising internal sensation and impaired awareness lasting under a minute, often clustering late morning.
The drop attacks tend to occur in short runs over two to three days, typically in the early evening after work, with quick recovery but occasional bruising.
The tonic-clonic events are usually nocturnal or in the early hours; he is sore and fatigued for the following day.
He denies clear photic triggers, and states the seizure frequency does not vary with welding versus non-welding days.
He continues his regular medication regimen as previously prescribed and has not missed doses.
He reports that hydration, regular breaks, and consistent sleep help him feel steadier, though not changing the overall frequency.
Examination in clinic today was unremarkable with normal speech, cranial nerves, tone, and coordination; gait steady.
Sleep has been variable due to shift changes at the workshop.

citations.text: Summary/Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with photosensitivity and startle-triggered events; early-years childcare worker in a high-noise, multitasking environment; sleep fragmentation due to interrupted midday rest breaks; history of mild anxiety symptoms related to workplace interruptions.
Medication: Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily.

Over the past three months they describe several episodes per week of brief generalised events, typically characterised by abrupt behavioural arrest with eyelid fluttering and loss of awareness lasting 10–20 seconds, occasionally clustering in the early afternoon when rest has been curtailed.
Colleagues have noted brief pauses and dropped tasks without warning, followed by rapid recovery.
There have been two morning episodes with sudden generalised jerks on awakening, leading to the patient dropping lightweight items but without injury.
The patient has no focal aura or lateralising features.
No tongue biting or sustained postictal confusion has been reported after the brief absence-type events.
Sleep is curtailed by early starts and the unreliable rest period at midday. Alcohol is minimal, and there is no recreational drug use.
They deny missed doses; a weekly pill organiser is used. There have been no recent infections or intercurrent illnesses.
MRI brain (2023) was normal.
EEG (2024) showed generalised 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges provoked by hyperventilation and intermittent photic stimulation, consistent with generalised epilepsy.
Routine bloods last month were normal including FBC and LFTs.
Patient currently experiences several episodes per week of brief generalised absence-type seizures, with occasional morning myoclonic jerks on awakening.
These episodes are more frequent on days when the midday rest break is interrupted.
Continue sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily for now; adherence is good and no adverse effects reported.

Safety advice reiterated for childcare duties: ensure another adult supervises during water play or ladder use; avoid carrying hot liquids during early morning periods when myoclonic jerks have occurred.

citations.text: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset, based on semiology and diary-linked patterns.

Present medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
2. Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed (intermittent use per patient)

According to the patient’s seizure diary app, events have clustered around sudden noises; over the past six weeks there were four brief focal-aware episodes and one focal-impaired awareness spell, typically occurring when exposed to abrupt auditory shocks (e.g., dropped crockery or unexpected alarms).
The diary time-stamps correlate with environments of heightened noise and suggest a pattern consistent with startle-triggered focal events rather than spontaneous occurrences.
The patient reports good adherence and stable lamotrigine serum levels previously.
Given the diary-documented noise-precipitated events and occasional breakthrough focal-impaired awareness, I recommend adding lacosamide as adjunctive therapy while maintaining lamotrigine.

Initiate lacosamide as follows: 50 mg at night for 1 week, then 50 mg twice daily for 1 week, then increase to 100 mg in the morning and 50 mg at night for 1 week, and thereafter 100 mg twice daily if tolerated. Continue this dose until review.
Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily unchanged for now.
Clobazam may remain as intermittent rescue for anticipated high-risk noise exposures, but we advised minimising routine use to avoid sedation.
The patient will continue to use the seizure diary app to log events, noting context (sound level, location, preceding stress, sleep quality) and any medication side effects.
We asked them to enable the app’s noise-trigger tagging feature to refine correlation analysis.
Safety: advised on avoiding hazardous activities when episodes are more likely (e.g., handling hot liquids during known noisy times).
No driving until seizure-free in accordance with DVLA guidance; we have reiterated current restrictions and the need to report any loss of awareness events.
Please arrange basic metabolic panel and ECG locally before uptitration to 100 mg twice daily to screen for any conduction concerns.
If well-tolerated with improved control, we will consider cautious clobazam de-escalation.
If breakthrough events persist despite lacosamide, we will discuss alternative adjuncts or video-EEG monitoring.

citations.text: He describes focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; events begin with a rising epigastric sensation and left arm tingling, followed by behavioural arrest, lip-smacking, and unresponsiveness for 60–90 seconds. Post-event confusion lasts around 20–30 minutes, often with headache and fatigue.

what were previously sporadic episodes have evolved into short bursts of activity linked to warm environments and sustained physical activity—particularly on school field afternoons and community fun-run days—after which he may have two to three events over a 24–48 hour period.
Outside these activity-linked bursts, he is now averaging one focal impaired-awareness seizure every 7–10 days.

No clear nocturnal convulsive events have been witnessed, though he wakes on some mornings with tongue bite indentations and myalgia suggestive of possible secondary generalisation overnight on two occasions in the last six weeks.
He has focal epilepsy. Onset was in 2019 following a mild traumatic head injury.
MRI (2021) demonstrated right mesial temporal T2/FLAIR signal change without clear volume loss
EEG (2022) showed right temporal interictal sharp waves.
Current anti-seizure medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily and Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily, both reportedly taken reliably.
He notes mood lability on Levetiracetam but prefers not to alter this while seizures are active.

there has been a clear deterioration over recent months, marked by an increase in seizure-related incidents and several episodes resulting in falls and minor injuries (forearm bruising and a superficial eyebrow laceration managed with steri-strips at a walk-in centre).
There is no recent alcohol excess; sleep has been fragmented due to childcare demands. No intercurrent infection reported.
I have requested an urgent repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and ambulatory EEG (72 hours) to capture both habitual focal events and any sleep-related activity.

citations.text: He describes focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; events begin with a rising epigastric sensation and left arm tingling, followed by behavioural arrest, lip-smacking, and unresponsiveness for 60–90 seconds. Post-event confusion lasts around 20–30 minutes, often with headache and fatigue.

what were previously sporadic episodes have evolved into short bursts of activity linked to warm environments and sustained physical activity—particularly on school field afternoons and community fun-run days—after which he may have two to three events over a 24–48 hour period.
Outside these activity-linked bursts, he is now averaging one focal impaired-awareness seizure every 7–10 days.

No clear nocturnal convulsive events have been witnessed, though he wakes on some mornings with tongue bite indentations and myalgia suggestive of possible secondary generalisation overnight on two occasions in the last six weeks.
He has focal epilepsy. Onset was in 2019 following a mild traumatic head injury.
MRI (2021) demonstrated right mesial temporal T2/FLAIR signal change without clear volume loss
EEG (2022) showed right temporal interictal sharp waves.
Current anti-seizure medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily and Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily, both reportedly taken reliably.
He notes mood lability on Levetiracetam but prefers not to alter this while seizures are active.

there has been a clear deterioration over recent months, marked by an increase in seizure-related incidents and several episodes resulting in falls and minor injuries (forearm bruising and a superficial eyebrow laceration managed with steri-strips at a walk-in centre).
There is no recent alcohol excess; sleep has been fragmented due to childcare demands. No intercurrent infection reported.
I have requested an urgent repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and ambulatory EEG (72 hours) to capture both habitual focal events and any sleep-related activity.

She describes intermittent nocturnal episodes over the past three months with post-event confusion on waking and occasional tongue-biting reported by her partner.
Clusters characterized by two focal impaired-awareness seizures; frequency unclear.
She is keeping a basic diary but cannot specify how often these occur, as some events may be unwitnessed.
She notes increased stress at work and irregular sleep.
No intercurrent illness, head injury, or new medications. Caffeine intake modest; no alcohol excess.
Menses regular; no clear cyclical pattern noted by her.
Examination today was unremarkable with normal speech, gait, and cranial nerve examination. Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular.
Implement structured seizure and trigger diary with partner corroboration to help clarify timing and frequency of clusters and any precipitants (stress, sleep loss).
Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily for now.
Arrange routine EEG and updated serum biochemistry including U&E, LFT, and levetiracetam level (for reference only).
No emergency department attendances.
She remains currently not driving by self-report.

Safety counselling reiterated, including bathing precautions and avoidance of heights or open water when alone. She remains off driving and understands to inform DVLA as appropriate per current guidance.

Refer to hospital family liaison for support regarding housing-related stressors that may be impacting sleep and seizure threshold.

citations.text: Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset.
They remain on long-term antiseizure therapy, with bone-health monitoring recorded elsewhere.

They report stereotyped focal aware episodes characterised by a brief rising epigastric sensation and déjà vu, occasionally progressing to impaired awareness with automatisms lasting 1–2 minutes, followed by mild post-ictal fatigue. There have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures reported. Seizure clustering is not described.
Importantly, the median inter-seizure interval ≈ six weeks, with the patient noting that stress and missed meals can shorten this interval, while regular routines appear protective.
Since the last review, there has been no intercurrent illness, sleep pattern is stable, and there is no clear relation to menses or alcohol. Work attendance is good and no injuries have occurred.
Neurological examination today was non-focal.
Driving status and SUDEP risk were discussed; no red-flag features such as prolonged impaired awareness, cyanosis, or trauma were reported.
Emergency care plans remain unchanged as seizures self-terminate within minutes and there has been no requirement for rescue medication.
We agreed to continue current management and to keep a detailed seizure diary capturing potential precipitants, duration, and level of awareness during events.
Investigations and bone-health reviews are being coordinated through existing pathways and are documented elsewhere.

citations.quote: Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures of unclear classification
prior routine EEG reported as non-diagnostic
past MRI (2012) described as structurally unremarkable without focal lesion
Occupational exposure: metalwork/welding with intermittent arc-light exposure; uses protective visor per workplace routine.
He works full-time as a welder/fabricator and reports good adherence to workplace safety, including visor and appropriate filters; no sustained photic provocation at work.
Reports no issues with arc flash given visor use; no sustained photic discomfort.
He notes regular sleep and reduced caffeine.
He withdrew from Clobazam on 12/Jan. At that time, he had 2 seizures, one precipitated by illness.
He has remained stable without seizures since.

Current medication:
- None for seizure prophylaxis at present
- Thiamine 100 mg daily (self-initiated)
- Omeprazole 20 mg once daily for dyspepsia
He reports occasional brief light-headedness under stress
but no witnessed events
no tongue-biting, no incontinence, and no post-ictal confusion since January.
No new injuries.
Investigations: Prior EEG (2014) non-specific.
MRI brain (2012) structurally normal.
Routine bloods today (FBC, U&E, LFT, B12, folate, TFTs) requested.
Given his current stability off Clobazam since January with no further seizures, we agreed to continue observation without initiating a new anti-seizure medication at this point.
Rescue: Buccal midazolam supplied previously, unused in the past year.
To maintain a seizure diary (including intercurrent illness, sleep disruption, and any welding shift variations).
DVLA guidance reiterated; he confirms ongoing compliance.

citations.quote: Diagnosis:
Seizure disorder of uncertain classification.
Current Medicines:
Carbamazepine modified‑release 400 mg twice daily
Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily
The patient reports a clear deterioration over the past two months.
Skipping meals triggers focal epileptic spasms, and this has become more frequent due to their shift pattern and restricted access to personal items at a cleanroom/data‑hall workplace.
They describe brief clusters of spasms lasting seconds, sometimes progressing to confusion if a full dose has been missed.
Workplace controls have delayed retrieval of rescue medication and refills on several occasions, leading to missed doses and a noticeable worsening of seizure control compared with earlier this year.
The patient must request escorted access to lockers and pharmacy deliveries, often resulting in delays beyond dosing times.
Meal breaks are occasionally curtailed or rescheduled at short notice during maintenance windows, coinciding with increased spasms on fasted days.
No alcohol or recreational drug use reported; sleep is fragmented on night shifts.
Maintain current doses for now while the above adjustments are implemented; we will reassess frequency and severity at review in 8–10 weeks.
If access and adherence improve but spasms persist, we will discuss alternative therapies at next clinic visit.

citations.exact_evidence: Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset. Patient has practiced aura recognition strategies and is able to describe prodromal warning features in her own words.

Current concerns: She reports that over the past three months she has had brief clusters of events described as “jaw tightness then body goes stiff on the right, and I can’t get words out”, typically lasting under a minute with rapid recovery.

Aura/ictal description (patient-reported): “A rising wave in my stomach, a metallic taste, and sounds feel far away for a few seconds. If I catch it early and sit down, it sometimes fades; if I’ve missed tablets, it goes into the jaw clamping and right arm stiffening.”

She links these to missed doses of anti-seizure medication and has kept a diary demonstrating a temporal association. Overall seizure pattern is best characterised as focal tonic with missed ASM doses.
No clear generalisation reported.
Postictal phase minimal; feels briefly foggy.
Investigations: Prior MRI (2016) reported no definite epileptogenic lesion. Routine EEG in 2019 showed intermittent left temporal slowing without epileptiform discharges. No recent bloods on file.
Medication: Lamotrigine modified-release 200 mg twice daily. Rescue: buccal midazolam 10 mg PRN prolonged events (not used in past year).
Reports occasional missed evening doses during late shifts.
Relevant history: No new neurological deficits. No recent head injury. Sleep fragmented due to work shifts. Alcohol minimal. No pregnancy plans. Family history negative for epilepsy.

Self-management: She has actively used aura recognition strategies learned at previous epilepsy nurse sessions and keeps a structured seizure and dosing diary on her phone. She has set phone alarms but reports these are sometimes ignored during busy shifts.
Examination (today): Alert, oriented, fluent speech. Cranial nerves intact. Motor and coordination normal. No focal deficits. BP 118/72, pulse 72 regular.

Plan:
- Reinforce adherence: patient to switch to a single daily dosing schedule if suitable after serum level review; she agrees to pharmacy blister packs and watch-based reminders to reduce missed doses.
Bloods requested: FBC, U&Es, LFTs, lamotrigine trough level in 1–2 weeks.

Safety: reiterated individual seizure triggers (sleep loss, missed doses) and advised continued use of aura recognition strategies, sitting/lying down when warnings occur, and maintaining diary entries.

citations.evidence: Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; prior nocturnal and daytime focal to bilateral tonic–clonic evolution reported in March
Current Medication: Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily; Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for clusters (not recently required)
Continue levetiracetam at current dose
He reports a sustained improvement in seizure control since adopting adherence supports (pillbox and smartphone reminders), which have markedly reduced missed doses and improved day-to-day stability.

For context, before this improvement: in March he had 5 seizures during sleep and 5 while awake; in April he had none in sleep and one while awake. Since then, there have been no events suggestive of seizures
he has not experienced aura-like symptoms, automatisms, or post-event confusion. He has also not required rescue clobazam.
Neurological examination today is unremarkable, and there are no adverse effects from levetiracetam.
He describes satisfactory sleep, reduced stress at work, and consistent medication timing aided by the pillbox and reminders.
Safety advice reinforced; no driving until formally cleared per DVLA guidance
Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

citations.quote: Summary: Ongoing seizures of uncertain classification; prior normal MRI; high caffeine intake acknowledged.
He reports historically infrequent events since last year, with improved routine and sleep hygiene.
He remained seizure-free for 8 months after starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, before experiencing a generalised tonic-clonic seizure three Tuesdays ago, preceded by a cluster of absences.
He describes the absences as brief episodes of lost time with staring and no awareness, occurring over the preceding weekend.
He began Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily via primary care after a telephone consultation in December.
Current medication: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily; Vitamin D 1,000 IU daily (self-purchased).
Adverse effects: Mild irritability in the first fortnight of Levetiracetam, settled without intervention.
He recalls an MRI head from 2019 reported as normal (no report available today).
No prior EEGs available in our system.
Routine bloods earlier this year were reportedly normal via GP.
Arrange: routine EEG and repeat MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) to update baseline investigations.
Blood tests via GP: FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, vitamin D, and Levetiracetam trough level for reference.
The generalised event occurred mid-morning at work; there was a tongue bite and post-ictal confusion for approximately 30 minutes. No injury was sustained.
Continue Levetiracetam at current dose for now; adherence reinforced.
He drinks high-caffeine energy drinks (3–4 cans most weekdays) and several strong coffees daily, particularly when working late.
There is no clear trigger other than increased workload and caffeine.
He acknowledges poor hydration and intermittent missed breakfasts.
Sleep is reported as 5–6 hours on weeknights with weekend catch-up.
No recent intercurrent illness. No new medications.
He denies alcohol misuse or recreational drugs.
Family history unremarkable for seizures.
No known drug allergies.

citations.text: Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification with increasing frequency and situational sensitivity.
Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily
Clonazepam 0.5 mg nocte as rescue for clusters (patient-reported, used sparingly)
Increase Levetiracetam to 750 mg twice daily and monitor tolerability and mood
Arrange urgent EEG and MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
Bloods today: U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D; Levetiracetam level if clinically indicated
I reviewed Emily Carter in clinic today following a clear deterioration in her seizure control over recent months.
She has started a new role in an open-plan office where strong and intermittently flickering lighting is present, and she has noticed more events in that environment.
As of this month she reports 2 seizure events; two in August, 7 in July and 2 in June during both sleep and wakefulness.

Events are described as brief episodes of behavioural arrest with loss of awareness, sometimes preceded by a rising internal sensation and flashing lights in her vision, occasionally progressing to bilateral stiffening and jerking lasting under two minutes, with post-event confusion and headache.
There is no tongue biting or urinary incontinence reported in the majority of episodes.
Her partner has witnessed two daytime collapses at work, both temporally associated with exposure to overhead flicker.
Sleep deprivation and stress appear to exacerbate frequency.
Adherence to current medication is good by pill count and patient report.
Neurological examination today is non-focal.
We discussed risks related to driving and safety-critical tasks; she is aware of the current guidance and will refrain accordingly.

Normal brain MRI (January 2024)
Current Medication: Levetiracetam 500 mg bd
He describes brief events characterised by a rising epigastric sensation, momentary déjà vu, and preserved awareness, occasionally followed by mild right-hand tingling without spread.
He reports a frequency of ≤ three simple partial seizures per week, usually clustering during weeks with late-night tournaments and irregular sleep.
There are no witnessed convulsions, tongue biting, or incontinence.
Post-event fatigue is mild and short-lived.

Since starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily in May 2024, he notes a reduction in intensity and duration of episodes, though frequency remains tied to sleep disruption and extended sessions at dual monitors with high visual load.
He denies mood change or irritability on current dose.
No alcohol misuse or recreational drugs.
Neurological examination today is normal.
Prior MRI brain (January 2024) was reported as normal.
A routine EEG (February 2024) was non-diagnostic.
We discussed the pattern of triggers, particularly irregular sleep and prolonged continuous screen time with limited breaks.
he currently reports preserved awareness and no events at the wheel, and he does not drive.
I remain happy to review sooner should the pattern change or if he wishes to consider ambulatory EEG during tournament periods.

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Primary generalised epilepsy

Present Medication:

1. Valproate (modified-release) 500 mg in the morning and 1000 mg at night
2. Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for clusters (per personalised plan)
3. Vitamin D3 1000 IU once daily
Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every 4 days.
Rescue medication was required once in June and twice in August.
The patient has increased VNS swiping, but there has been no overall rise in seizure frequency since the last review.
The patient has generalised epilepsy with occasional cluster patterns linked to sleep loss, particularly after late shifts or long-haul travel.
A personalised plan for cluster management is documented elsewhere and remains unchanged; the patient is familiar with when to initiate the rescue protocol and when to present to emergency services.

Vagus nerve stimulator in situ since 2023; the patient reports more frequent magnet swiping at aura onset and during early cluster periods. No adverse effects beyond transient voice change. Battery status checked at last device clinic: satisfactory.
No recent hospital admissions. Headaches post-cluster respond to simple analgesia and hydration.
The patient is exploring sleep hygiene strategies (fixed bedtime, reduction of caffeine after midday) and has begun using a sleep diary to identify triggers.
Continue current anti-seizure regimen without dose changes at this time, given stable overall frequency and effective use of the personalised rescue plan.
No change to VNS parameters today; continue magnet use at aura onset and during clusters as per training. Next VNS device check at routine device clinic in 3 months or sooner if concerns.
Safety advice reiterated regarding water precautions, heights, and operating machinery. The patient confirms understanding and has support at home.

citations.text: Over the last two months his seizure control has clearly deteriorated. He reports increasing social pressures at work and more frequent evenings out, with higher alcohol exposure than previously, which he recognises often precedes poor sleep and clusters of events.
He keeps a diary. In Feb he had four seizures during sleep and 3 while awake. In Mar he had 4 in sleep and 2 while awake.
Prior to this, he had gone several months with only occasional brief episodes, typically after late nights.

Current medicines:
- Carbamazepine modified‑release 400 mg twice daily
- Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily

Previous medicines (patient‑reported):
- Lamotrigine up to 100 mg twice daily (stopped in 2014 due to rash)
- Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily (self‑discontinued in 2013 because of weight gain and tremor)

Neurological examination today was unremarkable. Mood is strained; he reports sleep restriction, variable adherence when out socially, and alcohol intake most weekends. No new focal neurological symptoms between events.

Sam’s seizure burden has increased in the context of heightened social stressors, reduced sleep, variable adherence, and increased alcohol exposure. He feels the recent uptitration of levetiracetam may have coincided with more frequent events and irritability, though this is confounded by lifestyle factors. There is no single reliable non‑medication trigger identified beyond alcohol and sleep loss.

Medication: Maintain carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily for now. Reduce levetiracetam to 500 mg twice daily over one week to assess for behavioural side effects while monitoring seizure frequency. Begin lacosamide 50 mg at night for one week, then 50 mg twice daily ongoing.
Safety and lifestyle: Strongly advised strict alcohol moderation/avoidance, consistent sleep schedule, and adherence with dosing alarms.
Discussed interim driving and safety precautions given recent events, including avoiding swimming alone, working at heights, and operating machinery.
Monitoring: Sam will continue his seizure diary, noting alcohol, sleep duration, and doses taken. We will check U&E/LFTs in four weeks and review in clinic in eight weeks, sooner if events escalate.

Sumatriptan 50 mg as required for migraine (max 2 doses in 24 hours).
Sertraline 50 mg once daily.
The patient reports good adherence to medication with no recent missed doses.
Sleep remains irregular due to shift work, and they are working with their employer to stabilise schedules.
No recent head injuries, intercurrent illness, or new medications.
The app logs indicate a regular pattern of seizures twice every two weeks, typically clustering on days following consecutive late shifts.
Episodes are described as brief events with preserved awareness followed by fatigue and mild headache; there have been no injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence reported.
Partners’ observations, entered into the app on shared entries, corroborate the timing and brief duration.

citations.evidence: Summary/Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; history of traumatic brain injury; travel-related anxiety; osteoporosis; former alcohol dependence (in sustained remission); hypertension; hyperlipidaemia.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Carbamazepine (Tegretol Retard) 400 mg twice daily; Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening; Atorvastatin 20 mg once daily; Lansoprazole 30 mg once daily; Bisoprolol 5 mg once daily; Ramipril 5 mg once daily; Thiamine 100 mg once daily; Vitamin B compound strong one tablet daily; Calcium/Vitamin D supplements; Carbocisteine 750 mg twice daily; Emollients as required.
Bill describes stereotyped focal impaired-awareness episodes featuring a rising epigastric sensation, brief arrest of speech, right facial pulling, and post-event confusion for several minutes.
Importantly, he and staff document 5 or 7 focal onset seizures in three weeks during a recent period that included an episode while travelling by air.
Outside that three‑week window, there have been no further episodes for the last six weeks.
There has been no secondary generalisation, tongue biting, or injuries.
No clear intercurrent infection. Medication adherence is generally good; one evening dose of levetiracetam may have been late on the day of outbound travel.
He links this cluster to marked anticipatory and in-flight anxiety, sleep disruption the night before travel, and missed hydration.
Diagnosis reaffirmed as focal epilepsy.
Maintain current anti-seizure regimen without dose change, given six seizure-free weeks following the travel-related cluster.

Travel context: Bill acknowledges travel anxiety, particularly around security checks and take-off. He is open to structured preparation and timing medication around flight schedules. No panic attacks outside travel.
Bloods: request U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D, and carbamazepine level via practice in the next two weeks.
Bone health: continue calcium/vitamin D; DEXA recheck due next year.
He continues to abstain from alcohol and attends weekly wellbeing sessions.

citations.quote: Clinic Date: 11 December 2015
Since his last appointment there has been a notable deterioration in his condition.
He reports recent job loss from his banking role, with associated stress and disrupted sleep patterns over several weeks.
In the 24 hours prior to clinic he experienced multiple seizures in past day
described by him and his partner as brief episodes of loss of awareness with post-event confusion and marked fatigue
He also reports several near-miss episodes over the preceding week
including sudden staring and unresponsiveness lasting under a minute, followed by headaches and irritability
He is currently taking Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. and Clobazam 10 mg nocte, both of which he states he has taken regularly.
He denies missed doses
sleep fragmentation and early waking have worsened his daytime somnolence and concentration
He also notes increased anxiety since becoming unemployed, with occasional palpitations and a sense of impending events before the episodes.
There have been no clear triggers such as intercurrent infection or alcohol excess, though caffeine intake has risen.
No injuries were reported
On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech and cranial nerves. Motor and coordination were intact; no focal neurological deficits were identified.
the clear pattern of worsening events temporally associated with stress and sleep disruption
I have arranged urgent ambulatory EEG within the next 1–2 weeks
will request updated serum drug levels and routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC)
prompt contact if events cluster or if any convulsive episodes occur
He is aware of the DVLA guidance and understands he should not drive.

citations.text: Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness seizures; prior MRI unremarkable; interictal EEG previously normal.
Current Management: Ketogenic/modified diet (supervised by an external specialist dietetic team); diet details recorded in notes. No regular anti-seizure medicines at present.
Routine bloods (lipid profile, liver function, bicarbonate) via GP every 6 months while on diet.
He reports a history consistent with focal epilepsy, characterised previously by brief episodes of behavioural arrest with automatisms and post‑event confusion lasting a few minutes.

Since adopting the diet, he describes that seizure occurrences have not been happening, and there have been no witnessed episodes by family or colleagues. He denies new neurological symptoms. Sleep and hydration are reported as stable.
He is not currently taking lamotrigine or other anti‑seizure medicines.
I have asked that the external dietetic team continue to supervise macronutrient ratios and provide periodic summaries for our records.
Should any breakthrough focal events occur, we would reconsider pharmacological options and repeat EEG.

citations.text: He reports intermittent episodes over several years characterised by a brief prodrome of tightness across the chest, a rising internal "surge"
and an abrupt loss of awareness witnessed by his partner.
On most occasions there is bilateral hand fumbling and a brief vocalisation.
He does not recall the episodes and is usually confused for 10–15 minutes afterwards, occasionally with a dull bifrontal headache.
He denies tongue biting or incontinence. There is no clear motor convulsion described.
He keeps a diary and, based on this, the episodes are now occurring abs monthly, with intervening weeks free of events.
He links recent clustering to periods of poor sleep and heavy workload.
He typically consumes four to five large coffees by midday and often an energy drink during late meetings.
He and his partner have noticed that on days with very high caffeine use and reduced sleep, he feels more jittery and has experienced the prodromal "surge" without progression on two occasions.
He has reduced alcohol substantially and reports no recreational drug use.
He does not drive.
There is no family history of similar episodes.
He had a normal birth and development and no history of significant head injury or CNS infection.
His medications include ramipril for hypertension and vitamin D.
He is not on any antiseizure medication currently.
routine EEG one year ago reportedly normal
MRI brain without contrast five years ago for headaches was described as normal.
I have not yet reviewed the original images or tracings.
Cardiovascular review is unremarkable; no syncope, palpitations, or exertional chest pain.
Neurological examination today was normal, including cranial nerves, motor, coordination, and gait.
Sleep is fragmented during busy periods (often <6 hours).
Overall, there are paroxysmal episodes with impaired awareness and post-event confusion.
The semiology is not sufficiently specific to assign a definitive classification at this stage.
I have discussed prolonged EEG monitoring with home ambulatory recording as a first step, followed by inpatient video-EEG if required.
I will also arrange an MRI brain with epilepsy protocol to ensure high-resolution temporal lobe coverage
I have requested thyroid function and metabolic profile via his GP to exclude contributory systemic factors.
He will keep a simple log of daily caffeine amounts and sleep duration alongside his existing event diary so that we can correlate potential precipitants with episodes.
No treatment changes were initiated today pending further data.
